coredata.dc:title: Dual-mode colorimetric and fluorometric probe for ferric ion detection using N-doped carbon dots prepared via hydrothermal synthesis followed by microwave irradiation
coredata.dc:creator: Omer, Khalid M.
Sartin, Matthew
coredata.dc:description: Dual-mode colorimetry and fluorescence detection methods have gained more attentions in recent years. In this paper, a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode probe was constructed based on N-doped carbon dots (CDs) that was synthesized using hydrothermal heating followed by microwave irradiation. The CDs were purified using a polarity-based method followed by a size-based method. The CDs selectively form a purple-colored complex with Fe3+ ions accompanied by quenching of the fluorescence emission. The fluorescence emission of CDs was not affected by common metal cations and anions, but was only complexed and quenched by Fe3+ ions. Cyclic voltammetry and optical spectroscopy were used to measure the HUMO and LUMO levels of the CDs. The fluorescence quenching is due to the transfer of the photo-induced electrons from the excited state of the CDs to the LUMO of Fe3+ ions. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+ was examined by time-correlated single-photon counting. These results along with changes in the absorption spectra suggest a static fluorescence quenching mechanism. The CDs were used as a dual-readout probe for the colorimetric and fluorescent detection of Fe3+ ions in real samples. The lower limit of detection obtained using colorimetry was 0.5 μM (S/N = 3), while that obtained using fluorimetry was 50 nM. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs is pH independent between 2 and 9, which increases the working range for practical application. Furthermore, the CDs in aqueous solution are photostable, thermostable, and stable under high ionic strength, which makes the probe robust and rugged.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-mode probe
Carbon nanodots
Fluorimetric
Colorimetric
Ferric ion
Hydrothermal
Microwave
Double-purification step

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colorimetric and fluorometric assay of Fe3+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author would like to thank the Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research in Kurdistan region. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram showing the preparation scheme of CDs.
(A) TEM image of the CDs, inset is the histogram counting 150 nanoparticles. (B) XRD spectrum of the CDs.

High resolution XPS spectra for (A) survey spectra, (B) deconvoluted spectra for C1s, (C) deconvoluted spectra for O1s, (D) deconvoluted spectra for N 1s. The sample of CDs were drop casted on a piece of glass.
(A) Absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of CDs in aqueous solution. (B) Excitation-dependent fluorescence emission. Concentration of CDs 1.0 μg/mL.
(A) pH and ionic strength stability of CDs, (B) Photo and thermal stability of CDs. CDs concentration is 1.0 μg/mL.
(A) Cyclic voltammograms of CD film on glassy carbon at different scan rates. (B) Scan rate dependence of oxidation peak current. Supporting electrolyte is 0.5 M Na2SO4.

(A) Fluorescence decay of CDs measured by TCSPC in the presence (red) and absence (blue) of Fe3+. (B) Schematic diagram showing the photo-induced electron in the excited state of CDs transfer from the LUMO of CDs to the LUMO of Fe3+ ion, et: is the electron transfer. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Colorimetric results. (A) Absorbance spectra for serial dilution of Fe3+ with CDs solutions (B) calibration curve for series of solutions of Fe3+. The concentrations are labeled in the figure. (C) Digital photograph of CDs with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions. (D) Digital photograph of the CDs with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions.

(A) Fluorometric emission spectra for a series of solutions of Fe3+ ions. (B) Calibration graph of Fo/F versus concentration of Fe3+ ions, F o fluorescence intensity, when there is no Fe3+ ions, F fluorescence intensity with Fe3+ ions. (C) Digital photograph of quenching trend for different concentration of Fe3+ ions. (D) Effect of cations and anions on the fluorescence emission of the CDs. The concentration of metal ions and anions are 1.0 mM.
Determination of Fe3+ ions in tap water using colorimetric method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: “Found” is the amount determined by CDs a colorimetry reagent. It is an average of five replicates.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: “Measured” is the amount determined by AAS, It is an a average of five replicates.

head.ce:title: Dual-mode colorimetric and fluorometric probe for ferric ion detection using N-doped carbon dots prepared via hydrothermal synthesis followed by microwave irradiation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Sulaimani, Kurdistan Region, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Sulaimani, Kurdistan region, Iraq

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Texas at Austin, Austin, TX, 78712, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Texas at Austin, Austin, TX, 78712, USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Sulaimani, Kurdistan region, Iraq.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dual-mode colorimetry and fluorescence detection methods have gained more attentions in recent years. In this paper, a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode probe was constructed based on N-doped carbon dots (CDs) that was synthesized using hydrothermal heating followed by microwave irradiation. The CDs were purified using a polarity-based method followed by a size-based method. The CDs selectively form a purple-colored complex with Fe3+ ions accompanied by quenching of the fluorescence emission. The fluorescence emission of CDs was not affected by common metal cations and anions, but was only complexed and quenched by Fe3+ ions. Cyclic voltammetry and optical spectroscopy were used to measure the HUMO and LUMO levels of the CDs. The fluorescence quenching is due to the transfer of the photo-induced electrons from the excited state of the CDs to the LUMO of Fe3+ ions. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+ was examined by time-correlated single-photon counting. These results along with changes in the absorption spectra suggest a static fluorescence quenching mechanism. The CDs were used as a dual-readout probe for the colorimetric and fluorescent detection of Fe3+ ions in real samples. The lower limit of detection obtained using colorimetry was 0.5 μM (S/N = 3), while that obtained using fluorimetry was 50 nM. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs is pH independent between 2 and 9, which increases the working range for practical application. Furthermore, the CDs in aqueous solution are photostable, thermostable, and stable under high ionic strength, which makes the probe robust and rugged.

ce:section.ce:para: Dual-mode detection assays have gained much attention because they are more efficient and practical than single-mode assay. Several dual-mode detection methods have been reported, such as fluorescence/magnetic resonance imaging [1], colorimetry/surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [2], SERS/fluorescence [3], Colorimetry/fluorimetry [4]. Among these, colorimetry and fluorimetry, have received the most attention due to its sensitivity and relative simplicity [5].

Xu and Yan [6] have synthesized a fluorescent hybrid of (Eu3+/CDs@MOF-253) based on metal organic frameworks. Zhu et al. [7] have fabricated a nanohybrid by covalently linking green gold nanoclusters to the surface of silica nanoparticles embedded with red quantum dots for colorimetric and fluorimetric detection of lead ions. The abovementioned dual-mode detection assays suffer from several problems such as, low biocompatibility, complicated synthesis procedure, and high cost materials, which prevent their widespread application. Thus, it is beneficial to design a dual-mode colorimetric-fluorimetric assay with low cost materials, facile preparation, and high biocompatibility.

Fluorescent carbon nanomaterials offer a promising approach to achieving these goals, due to their unique properties and promising applications in various fields, such as analytical, environmental, material, and biological sciences [8–11]. Several methods have been used as detection methods including molecular absorption, molecular emission, and electrogenerated chemiluminescence [12–18].

Recently, carbon nanodots (CDs), emerging luminescent nanomaterials, have shown tremendous advantageous such as facile preparation, low-cost raw materials, photo and thermostability, biocompatibility, tunable emission, and high quantum yields [19–21]. Such characteristics make the CDs promising nanomaterials for wide range of applications, such as drug delivery [22], photocatalysis [23,24], electrocatalysis [25], sensors [26–28].

Recently, various analytical sensors were reported in literature as fluorometric probes for Fe3+ ions [29,30]. Shangguan et al. [31] reported 0.33 μM as a detection limit for N/P carbon dots derived from ATP. Wang at al [32] reported 0.22 μM as the lower limit of detection (LOD) using boron-doped carbon dots prepared from hydrothermal treatment of boric acid with glucose. Our group reported 50 nM as the LOD using a solvothermal method for preparation of carbon quantum dots derived from citric acid, urea, and phosphoric acid in DMF [27].

Similarly, the colorimetric (spectrophotometric) method has also received significant attention due to its advantage of direct visible detection without the need for complicated instruments [33]. One can exploit the advantages of both fluorimetric and colorimetric methods to construct dual-function detection method [34].

We have made use of both properties from carbon dots. Here, CDs were papered via hydrothermal-microwave methods followed by purification using a double-purification step (Fig. 1 ). The CDs were used as both fluorescence probe and colorimetric reagent for determination of Fe3+ ions, because Fe3+ ions can quench the CDs emission and make colored complexes with CDs. Selectivity and sensitivity of both detections as well as characterizations of CDs are discussed.

The chemicals used throughout this work were of analytical grade and used as purchased without further treatments. Deionized water was used for the preparation of the solution. 4-aminophenol was purchased from Aldrich (Aldrich-Sigma, USA). Fe3+ ions were prepared from FeCl3·6H2O (99%, Sigma-Aldrich). All other metal ions were of nitrate and chloride sources were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Quinine sulfate (Quinine hemi-sulfate salt monohydrate) was bought from Sigma-Aldrich.

Transmission electron microscopy, TEM (JEOL 2010 F) was used for size measurements. For TEM analysis, some drops of CDs solution were cast onto a carbon-coated copper grid and dried overnight at ambient temperature. FTIR spectra were recorded using a Varian 640 FTIR spectrophotometer (Palo Alto, CA, USA). Steady-state fluorescence measurements were recorded on a Flurolog-3 (Horiba, USA). Time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) experiments were carried out using the Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrophotometer, with an excitation wavelength of 371 nm (NanoLED), repetition rate of 1 MHz, emission wavelength of 470 nm, and integration time of 5 min. UV-Vis absorption spectra were collected on an Agilent spectrophotometer (Cary 6000, Agilent, USA). High resolution XPS spectra were recorded on a Kratos Analytical using Al Kα radiation as the excitation source. XRD spectra were recorded on an UltimaIV (Rigaku, Japan). Cyclic voltammetry was recorded on a CHI 660 (CH Instruments, USA). A conventional three-electrode setup was used, consisting of a graphite rod and Ag/AgCl as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. A 3 mm glassy carbon electrode modified with the CQDs was used as the working electrode, and the supporting electrolyte was 0.2 M KCl. To prepare the modified electrode, the bare glassy carbon was polished with slurries of alumina to create a mirror finish. It was then sonicated in acetone, ethanol, and H2O for 5 min each. The shiny surface of the electrode was checked under the optical microscope to ensure the mirror polish of the electrode. Then, in a small vial, 100 μL of the CD suspension was mixed with 100 μL Nafion 0.05%, and the mixture was mixed thoroughly for 3 min in a vortex. This solution was then drop casted on the surface of the glassy carbon electrode using a microsyringe and left to dry at room temperature. Photostability of CDs was investigated under a Xenon light (Full spectrum, including UV and visible), 100 W/cm2. A household microwave with a 750 Watt radiation source (Hamilton Beach, USA) was used for microwave irradiation.

The typical procedure for solvothermal CDs was: dissolve 0.50 g m 4-aminophenol in 20 mL H2O, sonicate the solution for 20 min, then heat for 20 h at 200 °C in a Teflon-lined autoclave. The resulting solution was centrifuged for 20 min at 14,000 rpm, then the clear solution was mixed with the same volume of CHCl3 in a separation funnel. The organic layer was separated and dried very slowly over a hotplate. After adding 20 mL of water, the CD solution was dialyzed against deionized water for 24 h using a 1000 MWCO dialysis membrane. The clear solution was then heated for 1 min under microwave irradiation (750 W). The resulting solution again centrifuged and dialyzed same as before.

The relative fluorescence quantum yield of the CQDs was calculated using a standard method known as William's method [35]. The integrated fluorescence intensity was plotted versus absorbance for several concentrations, and the gradients of the sample and a reference fluorescent material, such as quinine sulfate, were used to determine the photoluminescence quantum yield, QPL of 0.45 [36,37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorometric assay of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The detection procedures for Fe3+ ions were performed in aqueous solution at room temperature. The stock solution of Fe3+ ions (100 μM) was prepared by dissolving 16.2 mg FeCl3 in 0.05 M HNO3, then further diluted with deionized water to 1.0 L. For the determination of Fe3+ ions, 1 mL CDs solution (0.1 mg/mL) was mixed with different solutions of Fe3+ ions to make a serial dilution. For the colorimetric measurement, the absorbance spectra were recorded after 2 min of mixing. For fluorometric measurements, the fluorescence spectra were recorded after 2 min of mixing. Real samples of water for the spike recovery experiment were taken from laboratory tap water.

Hydrothermal, followed by microwave irradiation, was used to prepare the CDs. The resulting nanodots were purified based on polarity and size. Polarity-based separation was performed by solvent extraction, while size-based separation was carried our using dialysis. The double-step purification gives a narrow distribution of properties for the nanoparticles (Figure S1, supplementary information). Fig. 2 shows the TEM image of the purified CDs with the histogram (the inset) of the size distribution. Counting 150 nanoparticles for the histogram, 2.0–2.5 nm is the mean size of the CDs. The XRD spectrum (Fig. 2B) shows a broad peak around 25°, indicating the graphitic structure of the CDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was used to investigate the surface chemical composition of the CDs, as shown in Fig. 3 . It contains three main peaks: around 530 eV for O 1s, 282 eV for C 1s, and 400 eV for N 1s.

Spectra in Fig. 3B–D shows the high resolution deconvoluted spectra of each XPS peak. C 1s is composed of three peaks based on the peak fitting (Fig. 3B). The peaks are at 284.1 eV for CC, 286.2 eV for C-OH/C-O, and 287.7 eV for CO [38]. The two peaks fitting the O 1s band (Fig. 3C) were assigned to two different groups: 532 eV for CO eV, and 533.2 eV for C-O/O-H [39]. These results suggest that there is a relatively large number of oxygen groups on the surfaces of the CQDs, and there is a nitrogen group bonded to the CDs as well. The N 1s peaks at 399.90 and 402.10 eV shown in Fig. 3D indicate that nitrogen exists mostly in the form of N-(C)3 and N-H, respectively [40].

For carbon nanodots that are less than 5 nm in diameter, most of the XPS signal comes from the whole particle, both core and surface, rather than from the surface alone. The reason is attributed to the fact that size of the CDs (below 5 nm) is smaller than the depth of the XPS (which is about 15 nm).

The UV-Vis absorption and steady-state fluorescence spectra were collected to investigate the optical properties of both CDs. Fig. 4 A shows the absorption spectra of CDs in aqueous solution. These show a 280 nm absorption peak that is assigned to the π to π* transition of graphene sheets [41]. The steady-state fluorescence emission peak centered at 465 nm (blue emission), when excited at 350 nm. The excitation spectra showed one peak at 290 nm, and a shoulder at 325 nm. Excitation-wavelength-dependence was observed for CDs (Fig. 4B), as is common for carbon nanodots, that depend on sizes, surface state, and molecular state [21]. The ΦPL calculated relative to quinine sulfate was 20%. However, the ΦPL of CDs before the 1-min microwave irradiation was 15%. Thus, there is fluorescence enhancement caused by 1-min microwave irradiation.

Stability of the CDs is highly significant for real-fluorescence probe, especially under rigorous conditions, such as high ionic strength, extreme pHs, photostability, and thermostability. Fig. 6A shows that the fluorescence of the CDs is stable almost from pH 2 to 9, which is a practical range for most of chemical analysis. Above 9.0, the intensity decreases and the emission color changes to green, which indicates chemical changes on the surface of the CDs. The CDs are very stable at low ionic strength (0.01 M NaCl) and high ionic strength (2.00 M NaCl) (Fig. 5 B).

Full spectrum Xenon light (100 mW/cm2) was used to examine the photostability of CDs in aqueous solution, as shown in Fig. 5B. The fluorescence intensity showed 7% loss after 20 h of irradiation, and 17% loss after 30 h irradiation. The thermostability or heat effect experiment, was performed by heating the CDs solution in a water bath, or oil bath above 100 °C, for almost 10 min. The sample showed a 6% decrease from the original fluorescence intensity after 100 °C and up to 23% decrease after heating up to 150 °C. Here the reason might be due to the formation of a carbogenic core or aggregation of the CDs at higher temperatures. Photo and thermos-stable CDs enables increasing of shelf-life of CDs.

The electrochemical behavior of the CDs was examined in order to calculate their energy levels. Fig. 6 shows the cyclic voltammograms carried out at different scan rate for the CDs loaded on the surface of a glassy carbon electrode. The onset oxidation peak potential is at 0.70 V versus normal hydrogen electrode (NHE). The peak current was linearly dependent on scan rate (Fig. 6B), indicating the adsorption of the CD film on the glassy carbon. One might use this modified electrode, CDs on glassy carbon. To find the highest occupied molecular orbits (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), electrochemical measurement combined with optical measurement (Band gap) are used. The HOMO value can be calculated as follows [42,43].
(1) EHOMO in eV = – (Epox + 4.4)
From equation (1).
EHOMO in eV = – (0.7 + 4.4)
= – 5.10 eV

From the band edge of optical spectra, the energy gap (Eg) is 3.4 eV,
(2) ELUMO – EHOMO = Eg
ELUMO = Eg + EHOMO
= 3.4 + (−5.10)
= – 1.7 eV

The quenching mechanism is attributed to complexation of Fe3+ ions with CDs, as shown in Fig. 9A (blue line), accompanied by electron transfer from the LUMO of CDs, at −2.04 eV, to the empty d orbital of the Fe3+ ions, i.e. from LUMO of CDs to that of the Fe3+ ions, at −2.46 eV. Such an electron transfer is energetically favorable, and the complexation between the CD and Fe3+ ions facilitates this transfer process.

To gain further insight into the quenching mechanism, time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) experiments were used to examine the charge transfer and exciton recombination process of CDs in the before and after addition of Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 7 A, the fluorescence lifetime of pure CDs (blue line) is short, consisting of a 1.0 ns day component and 6.6 ns day component, based on a biexponential function fitting. This rapid decay indicates a fast exciton recombination process. After the addition of Fe3+ (red line), the fast decay component accelerates to 0.8 ns? The insignificant decrease of lifetime indicates that the quenching is static rather than dynamic. Fig. 7B shows the schematic diagram of the photo induced electron transfer process between the excited CDs and the Fe3+ ions, based on the energy levels diagram.

Addition of Fe3+ ions to the CD solution leads to quenching of the emission of CDs accompanied by forming the purple complex of CD:Fe3+, which shows an absorbance band centered at 515 nm (Figs. 8 and 9
). Fig. 8A shows the absorbance for pure CDs, pure Fe3+ ions, and the complex, CD:Fe3+. As one can notice, the complex spectrum is different from both CDs and Fe3+ ions, indicating the formation of a new complex. The calibration curve was plotted using the absorbance of a series of solutions of CD:Fe3+ with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions (Fig. 8B–D). The calibration curve was linear from 1.0 μM to 15 μM of Fe3+ ions with a lower limit of detection of 0.5 μM, at which the signal to noise ratio (S/N) is equal to 3.

Based on strong and quick fluorescence quenching of CDs after addition of Fe3+ ions, real-time fluorescence probe can be proposed. For that, the fluorescence quenching was monitored with addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ ions. Fig. 9A shows that the emission intensity of CDs decreases with increasing Fe3+ ions from 50 nM to 1000 nM. Fig. 9B shows a plot of a calibration curve between Fo/F (Fo is fluorescence intensity of pure CDs, F is the fluorescence intensity of the Fe3+ ions) versus Fe3+ ions. The signal was linear with 0.99 as the correlation coefficient. The calculated LOD was 5 nM, when S/N was 3. The absorbance of Fe3+ ions at 470 nm (Fig. 9A) is very small, making the self-absorption by Fe3+ ions negligible. Fig. 9C shows the digital photographs of CDs with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions.

Due to the complexity of the water matrix, one of the grand challenges is not only the sensitivity, but also the selectivity. Thus, selectivity of the fluorescence quenching of CDs by Fe3+ ions was studied. The fluorescence intensity ratio is plotted as F/F o , where F o and F are the fluorescence intensities of the CDs before and after addition of metal ions and anions, such as; Fe3+, Fe2+, Cr3+, Co3+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cd2+, SO4 2−, NO2 −, and PO4 3−. As shown in Fig. 9D, only Fe3+ ions caused significant quenching, while the effect of other metal cations and anions was negligible. This means the CDs are very selective for Fe3+ ions.
Based on the selectivity, ruggedness, robustness, and lower limit of detection, we used the CDs as a colorimetric reagent and as fluorophore for the real-time detect ion of Fe3+ in tap water.

For the colorimetry, the sample was spiked with 1.0, 5.0, 10.0 μM of Fe3+ ions. As shown in Table 1 , the spike recoveries were 102, 97, and 98%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD%) were 0.2, 0.7, and 1.2, respectively. For the fluorimetry, the sample was spiked with 200, 400, and 800 nM of Fe3+ ions. As shown in Table 2 , the spike recoveries were 101, 102, 102, with standard deviations of 0.3, 0.8, and 1.2, respectively. To confirm the accuracy of both methods, the amount of Fe3+ ions in water samples were measured using the standard method, Atomic Absorption Spectrometry (AAS). The results in Tables 1 and 2 show good agreement between the AAS and our methods. Based on the experimental results and the statics, the CDs can be used as a colorimetric reagents and fluorophores with a reliable selectivity, sensitivity, ruggedness, and robustness.

In summary, N-doped CDs were prepared using a hydrothermal method followed by photoluminescence enhancement via microwave irradiation. The CDs selectively combined with Fe3+ ions that led to quenching of the fluorescence emission of the CDs to form a purple complex simultaneously. Fluorescence lifetime measurements with absorbance spectra of non-combined CDs and combined to Fe3+ ions suggest the quenching mechanism is from both dynamic and static mechanism. A dual-read out probe was proposed that works as a colorimetric method and a fluorimetric probe. As a colorimetric reagent, 0.5 μM was calculated as the LOD, while 5 nM was calculated as the LOD when CD was used as a fluorescence probe. Both LODs are lower than the water quality standard for Fe3+ ions in drinking water (∼5 μM) by WHO, implying that our method has the potential to be used for real-time water analysis assays. Both methods were used for quantitative determination of Fe3+ ions in tap water, and both displayed statistically reliable results after verification using the spike recovery method and standard method. This dual-readout probe has the advantage of using either colorimetry or fluorimetry based on laboratory capability and required analytical sensitivity.
The authors declare that there is no conflicts of interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author would like to thank the Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research in Kurdistan region.
